1: A revelation of God: The groom's elaborate praise of the bride's beauty — 'how beautiful you are, my love! How beautiful!' — is the divine delight in the redeemed people. The repetition ('how beautiful ... how beautiful') is not poetic filler but intensity. God's delight in his people is not grudging or conditional: Zephaniah 3:17 describes YHWH exulting over his people with loud singing. The wasf form (descriptive poem from head to foot) communicates that the beloved is beautiful in every detail, not merely in general impression.
2: A revelation of God: Teeth like freshly washed sheep, each with its twin, none missing — the imagery of completeness and purity. The flock with 'not one missing' anticipates Christ's declaration: 'I have not lost one of those you gave me' (John 17:12; Song of Solomon 18:9). The Good Shepherd does not lose a single sheep; the Bride presented to Christ has 'not one missing.' The eschatological completeness is already real in the divine intention.
3: A shadow: Lips like a scarlet thread — the scarlet of covenant and redemption. Rahab's scarlet cord in the window (Joshua 2:18-21) is the OT's most explicit scarlet-as-salvation image; the Passover blood on the doorposts is its prototype. The beloved's lips are the color of covenant rescue. Her words — the 'lovely' mouth — are the praises of the redeemed, the confession of the lips that saves (Romans 10:9-10: with the mouth one confesses and is saved).
4: A shadow: 'Your neck is like the tower of David, built in courses of stone; a thousand shields hang on it, all of them warriors' shields.' The tower of David as armory — shields displayed as trophies of completed victories. The beloved's neck carries the history of war and triumph. The church's identity is constituted by what Christ has accomplished: the trophies of his cross (Colossians 2:14-15: disarming the rulers and authorities, he put them to open shame). The strength of the church is not her own but the victories already won and displayed.
5: A revelation of God: 'Your two breasts are like two young fawns, twins of a gazelle, feeding among the lilies.' The imagery of the twin fawns feeding peacefully evokes abundance, gentleness, and contentment. The pastoral scene — feeding in a place of beauty — is the Psalm 23 image: 'he makes me lie down in green pastures' (Song of Solomon 4:2). God's delight in his people is marked by this same combination of protective strength and tender care. The fawns need no armor; they are in safe pasture.
6: A shadow: 'Until the day breaks and the shadows disappear, I will go to the mountain of myrrh and the hill of frankincense.' The mountain of myrrh is the mountain of Christ's sacrifice — Golgotha, where the King gives himself. Myrrh and frankincense are both burial spice (John 19:39) and incense of the holy place (Exodus 30:23, 34). The groom's willingness to go there — before the day fully breaks — is the self-giving love of Christ who 'went to the mountain' of Calvary while it was still the dark hour of the world.
7: A direct citation of this theme: 'You are altogether beautiful, my love; there is no defect in you.' Paul quotes the structural equivalent in Ephesians 5:27: 'that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, that she might be holy and without blemish.' The 'no defect' (mum in Hebrew — the same word used for Levitical sacrificial purity, Leviticus 22:21) is the standard Christ applies to the church in his sight. This is the most direct application of Song language to the Christ-church relationship in the NT.
8: A shadow: 'Come with me from Lebanon, my bride; come from Lebanon. Come down from the peak of Amana, from the summit of Senir and Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards.' The call to leave the dangerous high places and come with the beloved is the gospel's call to leave the world's kingdoms for the kingdom of Christ. 'Come out of her, my people' (Revelation 18:4) uses the same structure. Lebanon with its wild, dangerous heights is the world outside the protective love of the Beloved.
9: A revelation of God: 'You have stolen my heart, my sister, my bride; you have stolen my heart with a single glance.' The double address — 'sister, my bride' — combines kinship (covenant solidarity) and marriage (intimate love). The church is both family and bride to Christ: 'whoever does the will of my Father in heaven is my brother and sister and mother' (Matthew 12:50) and 'the Bride, the wife of the Lamb' (Revelation 21:9). A single glance of faith captivates the heart of God — this is the theology of grace: the smallest turning toward Christ draws the full response of divine love.
10: A revelation of God: 'How beautiful your love is, my sister, my bride! How much better is your love than wine.' The reversal: in 1:2 the bride said 'your love is better than wine'; now the groom says 'your love is better than wine.' The divine delight in the love of his people is genuine: God values the love of his people. John's vision of the lampstands (Rev 2-3) with the threat of removing the lampstand from the church that has 'abandoned the love you had at first' (Revelation 2:4) shows this is not a poetic compliment but a theological truth: God treasures the love of his people.
11: A revelation of God: 'Honey and milk are under your tongue; the fragrance of your garments is like the fragrance of Lebanon.' The honey and milk under the tongue — the language of the promised land (Exodus 3:8; Song of Solomon 13:5) — applied to the bride herself. The church is the place where the promised land's abundance now resides. The NT equivalent: the community of the Spirit where the fruits of the Spirit (Galatians 5:22-23: love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control) are present. The fragrance of Lebanon is the fresh purity of a life lived before God.
12: A shadow: 'My sister, my bride, is a garden locked, a spring sealed, a fountain closed.' The locked garden and sealed spring — the church as a place that is exclusive to Christ and not open to all. Paul expresses the same exclusivity in 2 Corinthians 11:2: 'I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ.' The church's distinctiveness in the world — her 'sealedness' — is not arrogance but covenant loyalty: she belongs to one Lord alone. The sealed spring will open when the Beloved enters (Song of Solomon 4:16).
13: A shadow: The spice garden — pomegranates, henna, nard. The incense spice catalogue (Song of Solomon 4:13-14) parallels the Tabernacle incense of Exodus 30:23-25: myrrh, cinnamon, calamus, cassia. The bride as a garden of holy incense spices evokes the holy place where God dwells. The NT applies this to the church: 'we are the aroma of Christ to God' (2 Corinthians 2:15); the prayers of the saints are incense before the throne (Revelation 5:8). The garden-bride is the dwelling-place of the divine fragrance.
14: A shadow: Nard, saffron, calamus, cinnamon, frankincense, myrrh, aloes — the full incense catalogue of Exodus 30. The deliberate parallelism with the Tabernacle incense identifies the bride as a walking sanctuary, a living holy-of-holies. The NT's fulfillment: 'do you not know that you are God's temple and that God's Spirit dwells in you?' (1 Corinthians 3:16); 'you yourselves like living stones are being built up as a spiritual house, to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ' (1 Peter 2:5).
15: A shadow: 'A garden fountain, a well of fresh water, streams flowing from Lebanon.' The enclosed garden of the bride has an inexhaustible water source. Jesus applied this imagery to himself: 'If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink ... as the Scripture has said, "Out of his heart will flow rivers of living water"' (John 7:37-38). The bride as a garden-fountain is, in the NT, the community from which living water flows to others — the church as the place where Christ's life is distributed to the world.
16: A shadow: 'Awake, O north wind, and come, O south wind! Blow through my garden and carry its fragrance abroad.' The bride's prayer for the winds to carry her fragrance abroad — the petition for the Spirit to disperse what is gathered in the closed garden into the wider world. This is the structure of Pentecost (Acts 2:2: a sound like a mighty rushing wind) and of the church's mission: 'you will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you will be my witnesses ... to the end of the earth' (Acts 1:8). The garden opens to the world when the Spirit blows.
